speaker: interviewer
text: --13, 1995, Tape Number 5. OK, so you're in Sweden now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're living in Stockholm with your sister, and you're trying to get into the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you petitioned-- which relatives did you have here?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had some cousins here who came out a long, long time ago, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: On your mother's side?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and as I told you, we were found through the Red Cross. And they sent us the affidavit, but it took-- the Hungarian quarter was very, very bad, so we decided if we can come here to the United States, we would go to-- to be with our brother to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was very happy to hear that we're going to move there, and he got ready for us. And he says, oh, you're going to have a wonderful job here, and so and so. And in 1949, of December, we got a note that we got the visa to come here to America. So what we you going to do now? How are we going to let my brother know that we're not going to go there anymore? So-- and we ask our friends, what would you do in this situation where we are? They all said, go first to America, because from there, you could always go anyplace. But if you're going to go, you know, to Israel, then it's a little bit harder to go. So we took the advice, and we came over here to the United States. So we took a boat, a Swedish boat, and that was in March of 1950. Of course, it was a windy month. It took us two weeks to get here, but I tell you, it was worth it, all the wind, and the seasickness, and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the bad part was to write to our brother that we not going to come there. And he kind of felt very bad about it, because he looked forward to be reunited. Well, anyway, our relative-- we arrived here on the 15th of March, and the relatives were waiting for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? Where did you arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York City, with the boat. And they brought us up to Bridgeport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to go through any official customs, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the usual, the usual thing. Nothing special, because we had the papers and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: No immigration?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't-- I mean, they looked through the papers, yes, they do. Naturally, they do. And so we came to Bridgeport and--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were living in Bridgeport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And the West End, they called the section, and it was like a little hometown. When you went out on the street, you could only talk, you know, here speak Hungarian language to speak, but it was good in a way. And we were trying to-- we arrived on a Tuesday, and Thursday night we signed up for night school to learn the English language. So then, the next thing was to look for a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came with very little, you know. We stayed with our relatives for a short time. And then they had some house that somebody moved out, and they fixed up a three-room apartment for us, a bedroom, a living room, and a kitchen. We had to share the-- the bathroom with some other people in the hallway, but that was OK. We'd been through worse things than that. But we had a bathtub and running water, so that was OK. And so the next thing was to still look for a job. And there was-- you know, I said, well, I have a trade, I should have no problem. So my cousin took me to her hairdresser and asked him if he would, you know, take me in as a worker. So he explained he can't, because I have to get a Connecticut license. I can't work in my field.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the meantime, my sister found a job again in a factory sewing dresses. So the next best thing was for me, too, to do that, to go there and work together. And then I went to night school. And it took a long time, because at night, you could just put in so many hours. So then my sister told me that I should stop working, and she going to take care of me, and I should go during the day to the hairdressing school. And this way, you know, I would get through faster. So that's what I did. I went during the day, and I went during the night, just so I could finish it up very quickly. And I did, in two years. It took two years to finish up.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, I picked up pretty well the English language, because when you're younger, and you're between people who speak English, and you want to learn it, you know, you could learn faster. And so when I get my license, then I went to work in my field. And in the meantime, I met my husband, and he helped me--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody introduced me to him, and he helped me a lot with studying from the book, because I had to study very hard. The practical work was very easy for me, but from the book it was kind of hard. So he helped me with questions, quizzing-- you know, quizzing me? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Quizzing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Quizzing, yeah. And after my sister met somebody, was introduced from New York a young man. Well, actually, he was much older than she, and they got married in 1953 of December. And I got married in 1954 of June, in a half a year after her. So here in--

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did we? OK, so we're at the point where you're working, and you're studying for your hairdressing. And you've gotten married now, and your sister's gotten married. And this is-- yours is in June of 1954?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you've married Andrew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I married Andrew, and we moved to a different place. Actually, we moved before, when my sister was still here. But then I stayed there by myself in the apartment, a different part of Bridgeport, in the Black Rock section, a nice section. It was a lot of Swedish people there. I could have spoken Swedish that time, you see? And it was too late. And-- so then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we started a family after a while. I got pregnant with Gerald, our first son. And I had to quit my job, and I came--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that that he was born?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1957, in January. And then my trade changed. I came to be a house engineer. And I had to take care of everything that goes with that. And I was with the children. And I mean, those days, in the '50s and '60s, the mother stayed home with the children, to bring them up. Today's a different [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] story, but it was nice. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You had another child after Gerald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, after four and a half years, I got pregnant again with Nancy, a little girl, and that completed, you know, our family. And so the life here in America, you know, if you want to be somebody, you could be it. You had the chance. I mean, here I am, I came really with nothing, absolutely nothing I came here with. And because I worked so hard, because I worked so hard, and I wanted to be somebody, and work myself up, I had a chance. And I'm really very happy that we did come to the United States, because this is a country where-- I don't think there is any other country like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had some thoughts that you wanted to share with us--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: About your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I-- I wrote down a few thoughts, because certain things you just forget if you don't mark it down, and I'd like to just read it for you quickly. We all have something in our past we want to forget, but I have something I cannot forget. The number is 6145, brutally tattooed on my arm 49 years ago, an indelible keepsake of the terror which filled my life during the Holocaust. I don't want to forget my memories of hate, brutality, and fear that I witnessed as a captive Jew. Those memories are vivid. How can anyone understand what it was like for a young girl to arrive at a cold concentration camp, where young and old were divided, men and woman kept apart, mothers to the left, children to the right. We promised to be reunited on Sunday. That just wasn't so. That was the first many of lies, and beginning of persistent death, crawling through the air as the flames from the chimneys escaped from the crematorium, with the acid, unbearable smell of burning flesh. But we did bear it, with my sister and many other prisoners, as long as we could. Our heads shaven, we stood next to one and another, in a large, large crowd, waiting. We didn't-- we did not recognize each other. They told us we would see our mother again, that she was just sent to take a shower. But they didn't take a shower. Our mother was dead. I remember after that, I really didn't care what happened. All of the Auschwitz prisoners were doped. As soon as we got our first meal-- black coffee and black hard bread-- it lessened the fear and numbed us to what was happening around us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only who was there can ever know what it was like to be eating for one day at Auschwitz. How do I describe fear? How do I describe hunger? Hunger can-- could be so bad that your pit of your stomach hurt. You would give anything for a potato or a slice of bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: How do I describe living with lice on your body and on your clothes, the fear, the selection, and the appeals to see Mengele every day, and mostly, mostly dead, the gas chamber. I would also like to just say this-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that I remember of that moment how I-- this is about liberation. I remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ask her to turn the pages very gently. Because I hear that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Turn the pages very gently.

speaker: interviewee
text: Liberation. I remember of that event-- how can I ever forget? It was May 2nd of 1945. It was told by the soldiers who liberated us with the Red Cross armband. We had no choice. Our eyes were open to the end of a nightmare. What we went through, we were alive, but our families and friends were dead. There was no special celebration or speeches, only emptiness, nothing ours. Better perhaps, because we were so weak, sick, riding two weeks in a cattle car. We had no families to return to, really just [NON-ENGLISH] in Yiddish, guide had vanished. I personally never had a chance to go back to my country town, and see how I would have-- would be received, or if someone would recognize me or know my name after all these years. My sister and I had a dream, perhaps we should go back to Hungary and see our little village where we were from, and visit our grandmother's grave and our little sisters' graves. Fate changed everything in my life. My dear sister Frieda passed away. My life was shattered, and everything was put on hold. Perhaps it would be good to see our village and see the home where we once lived in more happier days. I wish I could-- it will be 50 years ago that I did not see my grandmother's grave. My memories go back to Auschwitz-Birkenau, where I lost all my family. I would have the strength, if I would have the strength, I would like to walk there and pick some flowers from between the tall grass, and bring back some ashes and dark gray stone. I just like to, for conclusion, to tell you why I feel like I wanted to tell my story to you. For me, it is very important to tell my story what I endured during the Holocaust, what I saw in the concentration camp, and what I lived through to tell. For so many years, the survivors could not talk about the pain and the suffering we felt. When I went to Israel with my sister Frieda in June of 1981, to the very first world gathering of the Jewish survivors, that was the most memorable experience we had. We were welcomed by President Yitzhak Navon, the Prime Minister Menachem Begin, and Mayor Teddy Kollek of Jerusalem, Benjamin Meed, Ernest Michel, and many, many other dignitaries. I just mentioned a few. They all asked and begged the survivors that we should talk about our experience, and the hell, and the horrible things we went through. It is our duty to tell our story to the world, that the Holocaust did happen, and we should teach the children in school, and the public, and everyone we know, the Holocaust must be remembered. Because if we forget it, it could happen again. For me, a new world opened up after four days gathering in Israel. When I came back, we were invited to a different organization to speak, and my heart felt a little lighter to see that people were interested in our story, what we had to say. Today I feel very strongly to tell my story, and feel obligated to tell about the Holocaust, that innocent people should never, ever should experience the horrible thing what we had to see and live through. We have to document it and teach the children and everybody, so the world should know. It is so very important, because we are getting older. And the second generation, our children, have to carry on our work what we started to tell, that the Holocaust did happen. I was there. I have proof in book. Our picture's in it, with my sister. My mother is holding my little niece in front of the crematorium. My father is there, too, with my cousins. They were all killed, because they-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they were all killed not because they committed a crime. The only reason was because they were Jewish. I only-- I am only sorry that I was not able to talk about my experience to my children when they were growing up. I never wanted them to know what I experienced as a young girl in the concentration camp. Perhaps it would be too soon to talk at the beginning. The scar was very deep, and the survivors was not ready to open up. This is something so deep inside of me that it-- that it will stay with me forever. But this is our duty to tell the story, and we must remember that the Holocaust did happen. And we have to hand it down to the next generation-- our children, their children, and all generations-- that something like this should never, ever happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Elizabeth, for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Elizabeth, will you introduce us to your family? If anyone wants to say anything, feel free, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband Andrew, and we were married just 41 years last June. And this is our daughter, Nancy, and our son, Gerald, and our little granddaughter-- I don't know where she is. Her name is Ashley. And our son-in-law, our favorite one, I'm sorry he couldn't be here. But they might show a picture of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Danny. Danny. So that's my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyone want to say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to say Ashley here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe Ashley would like to say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Come on, sweetheart. We want to see your face. You Want Grandma to give you what I promised?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't egg her on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me see how big you can smile.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pretend we're in the photo studio.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's going to say cheese. He's going to say Barbie.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then a Barbie's going to appear, and then he's going to-- Can you say hello to your grandma? Can you go shake your grandmother's hand? [SQUEALS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Stop it. Shake your grandmother's hand.

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you like the most? What do you like the most? [INAUDIBLE]. Come on, you're my sweetheart. You're my best little granddaughter. [INAUDIBLE] What's that? What's the name of it? ASHLEY AMES Bubble Angel Barbie. Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bubble Angel Barbie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Is that what you want?

speaker: interviewee
text: But daddy was going to buy me it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'll get you another one.  How's that? OK? We'll think of another name. All right? All right? You're a good girl, OK? Very good. Yeah, this picture was taken in Hungary. I don't remember exactly what year, in the school. And I'm sitting from-- next to the teacher from the left. And this tall girl from the left, the second, she was my very, very good friend, and would have been my sister-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is in the back row.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's in the back row. This picture was taken in Hungary in the school. I don't remember the year. I'm sitting right next to the teacher, to the left. And in the back row, the second girl was my very good friend, and was engaged to my brother. And I also had many other friends in the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Aranka. My cousin is in this picture, to-- to the right, kneeling, on the very end. His name is Herschell Hoffman. He went to the same class with Julie Kramer. I met her here in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is, from the teacher, the third to the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the girl who was going to marry your--

speaker: interviewer
text: Aranka.

speaker: interviewee
text: Aranka. Her name is Aranka. She is this second from--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aranka Stern. This is my sister, Frieda, Egri Salomon. She saved my life, actually, in the concentration camp. She was like my mother, my sister, and my very, very best friend. And this picture was taken just in 1942 in home in Hungary. This is my father, Benny Egri. He's-- this picture was taken when he was in the labor camp in Hungary. It must have been in 1943, perhaps, or '42. I don't remember exactly the date. This is and we have many pictures from us in this book, because when we were coming into Auschwitz-Birkenau, they were taking pictures of the Hungarian Jews, the SS men. And [NON-ENGLISH], the girl who found the album after the liberation, was holding onto it. We didn't know about this book till 1982. That's when we find out that we were in this book. This picture was taken out from This is a picture when we arrived in Auschwitz. My aunt, the tall woman, is standing on the far left. Next to her is our neighbor. Her name is Aunt Rose. Then Zseni. Then you could see only my face, and my sister right in front of me in the front. Her hair is on her forehead. In the white kerchief, the girl, her name is Aranka, and she was engaged to my brother. This is where we were standing already when we were separated from our family to be processed later on.
